summary: My grandmother was born in San Jose, California, and she worked the land there before the war. It was one of the places where her family moved around to find work in the crops. San Jose was part of her early life and shaped her as a Nisei woman before the internment disrupted everything.

My grandmother met her husband Frank in Campbell, California. Both were Nisei and members of the Japanese American Citizens League, which might have been where they met. Campbell was important as the place where their relationship began, even though it was complicated by arranged marriages and family disapproval.

Before the war, my grandmother worked the land in Watsonville, moving wherever there were crops to harvest. It was part of her life as a hardworking woman who took on farm labor like many Japanese Americans at the time. Watsonville was one of the agricultural communities that shaped her experience.

My grandmother was a member of the Japanese American Citizens League, and I believe that's where she met my grandfather Frank. Both were Nisei and involved in the organization before the war. The League was part of their social life and community engagement before everything changed with the internment.

World War II was the turning point that sped up my grandparents' marriage and led to their internment. They were quickly separated and sent to different camps, and their lives were forever changed by the war. It was a time of fear, loss, and upheaval for our family and many others.

My grandmother and Frank were interned at Heart Mountain Relocation Center after being held at Santa Anita. They took a five-day train ride there, and my grandmother wrote about the landscapes she saw but couldn't freely explore. Heart Mountain was where they lived during the war and where my grandmother gave birth to her first child.

Frank's family was mostly interned at Gila River War Relocation Center, but Frank himself went with my grandmother to Heart Mountain. The families were spread out across different camps, which added to the difficulty of their internment experience. Gila River was part of the broader network of camps that affected our family.

Some members of Frank's family were interned at Poston War Relocation Center, while he was at Heart Mountain with my grandmother. The family was scattered across camps, which made it hard to stay connected. Poston was one of the places where our relatives were held during the war.

Before going to Heart Mountain, my grandparents were at the Santa Anita Assembly Center. My grandmother wrote in her diary about a riot that happened there, describing it in tiny cursive because she feared her writings might be confiscated. Santa Anita was the first stop in their internment journey and a place of tension and fear.

My family was deeply affected by the Japanese American internment during World War II. My grandparents were separated and sent to different camps, and my grandmother worked harvesting sugar beets in the camps to help with labor shortages. The internment was a time of loss, especially when my grandmother's first child died in camp due to inadequate medical care.

My grandmother was very American in many ways, and she danced the Charleston back in the 1940s. It showed her tomboy spirit and how she embraced American culture despite her Japanese heritage. The Charleston was part of her youthful social life before the war disrupted everything.

While interned, my grandmother worked harvesting sugar beets because there was a shortage due to men being drafted. It was like prison labor, but she signed up to help with the crisis farmers were facing. This work showed her progressive and feisty spirit even in difficult circumstances.

I went back to Japan for the first time recently, and it was a strange experience because I felt like an outsider there. Japan is so homogenous and controlled, and I felt like my American ways were too loud or big. Still, some things felt familiar, like the slow, measured pace of life and the way people talk, which reminded me of my Japanese heritage.
